label: Cruthu Foundation Way2Work
description:
Way 2 Work Ireland (W2W) is a not-for-profit organisation fully funded by Tusla the Child and Family Agency offering supports such as individual and tailored assistance through a mentor / mentee, training, and employment programme. W2W Ireland  deals specifically with young people (aged 16-23) who have been referred by Tusla. W2W was established on the 1st of January 2019 to work in partnership with Tusla Child and Family Agency and private enterprises nationwide.  

Mission
To support young people’s ambitions and create opportunities for all young people irrespective of privilege or background.

Vision
To create a sustainable solution for the provision of apprenticeships and employment by facilitating engagement between state agencies and the private sector. This is fully supported by our mentorship and training programmes for successful outcomes.
name: Cruthu Foundation Way2Work

label: Special Olympics
description: Special Olympics Ireland, provides year-round sports training, athletic competition, and developmental programmes for almost 7000 athletes annually. Giving our athletes continuing opportunities to develop physical fitness, demonstrate courage, experience joy and participate in a sharing of gifts, skills and friendships with their families, other Special Olympics athletes and the community. Special Olympics Ireland is proud to be an all island programme made up of 5 Regions which govern different geographical areas and is supported by a  team in a central office. They are Connaught, Eastern, Leinster, Munster and Ulster Regions.
name: Special Olympics

label: First Fortnight
description: First Fortnight Ltd is an Irish charity dedicated to challenging mental health stigma through arts and cultural action. Established in 2009, the organization aims to make the first two weeks of each year synonymous with mental health awareness by hosting the First Fortnight Festival, an annual event featuring a diverse range of artistic performances, workshops, and discussions. Awardee supported by Rethink Ireland in various impact areas.
name: First Fortnight

label: Tacu Family Resource Centre
description: Tacú FRC promotes and operates a family resource centre and development programme, which  acts as a focus and catalyst for development for the community of Ballinrobe and surrounding areas
name: Tacu Family Resource Centre

label: TUSLA
description:
Public Sector Partner & Funder:TUSLA is Ireland’s national agency responsible for improving wellbeing and outcomes for children. It oversees child protection, family support, and early intervention services. Child and Family Agency, collaborative safeguarding efforts.

In collaboration with First Fortnight, TUSLA supports safeguarding and referral processes for vulnerable children receiving creative arts therapy.
name: TUSLA

label: Community Foundation Ireland
description:
Community Foundation Ireland is a philanthropic organisation that connects donors with charitable causes across the country. It supports long-term social change by funding community-based initiatives, including projects that promote mental health, social inclusion, and the arts.

Philanthrophic hub, NGO, working on projects, one of which is Begin Together,  a year-long initiative to provide group creative arts therapy for children and young people who are experiencing or at risk of homelessness in Ireland. The project will focus on delivering 60 therapy sessions across three key locations in Dublin and Meath.
name: Community Foundation Ireland

label: Blue Box Creative Arts Centre
description:
Mental Health Service Providers & NGOs: Blue Box is a Limerick-based therapeutic centre providing creative arts therapies to children and young people in schools and communities. It is known for its focus on trauma-informed, early intervention. 

Limerick-based therapeutic partner. As a therapy location and local partner, it enhances First Fortnight’s reach and impact in the region.
name: Blue Box Creative Arts Centre

label: Knocklee House (Kerry Recovery Centre)
description:
Knocklee House is an 8-bed supported facility in Tralee providing semi‑independent living with tailored care plans and intensive support for people experiencing homelessness with mental health challenges.

Knocklee House is a strategic partner in First Fortnight’s efforts to expand creative therapies to vulnerable adults through trusted local services.
name: Knocklee House (Kerry Recovery Centre)

label: St. Brigid’s Family Resource Centre (Tralee)
description:
St. Brigid’s FRC is a community hub in Tralee that delivers a wide range of supports including parenting classes, youth services, and wellbeing programmes. 

St Brigits is a Partner of First Fortnight for upcoming Kerry-based service. As a future therapy site for First Fortnight, it plays a key role in local outreach, referrals, and engagement with families in need.

The centre offers a range of family and community supports, including parenting programmes, a parent and toddler group, full-day childcare for children aged 1-12 years, adult education courses, support groups, assistance for the unemployed, and advice and information services. Its primary aims are to enhance the economic, social, cultural, and environmental development of the area by assessing local needs and delivering tailored programmes, as well as to combat disadvantage by improving family functioning.
name: St. Brigid’s Family Resource Centre (Tralee)

label: NOVAS
description:
NOVAS is a voluntary organisation that supports vulnerable individuals experiencing homelessness, addiction, or mental health issues. Active across Ireland, it offers housing, health services, and outreach.

NOVAS helps people experiencing homelessness move into stable accommodation and sustain tenancies by delivering intensive home‑based support and move‑on services.

NOVAS is a key partner in embedding First Fortnight’s therapies within trusted local care frameworks, especially in Limerick and Kerry.
name: NOVAS

label: Involve CLG
description:
nvolve Youth and Community Services was founded in 1972 as a voluntary committee, and established as a limited company with charitable status in 1988.  Involve went through a rebranding and reconfiguration of the organisation during 2011, with the head office and the organisations name being officially changed from NATC and launched by the then Minister for Children and Youth Affairs, Francis Fitzgerald on July 8th 2011. The Organisation’s original function was the representative body for Senior Traveller Training Centres with Government Departments and other Statutory and Voluntary Organisations, the last of these centres being phased out in 2012.

Involves history is embedded in education and training. Today, Involve is a youth, community and communications organisation working primarily with members of the Traveller community. It is the main provider of youth work services to young Travellers in Ireland.  As part of the delivery of this service we also focus on young people from marginalised backgrounds.
name: Involve CLG

label: Agirre Lehendakaria Center
description: Agirre Lehendakaria Center is a Social Innovation Laboratory specialized in developing systems thinking capabilities to address complex societal challenges with a developmental evaluation approach. In contrast to the traditional linear and single point evaluation approach, which applies a logical framework to implement activities that are designed to respond to specific, pre-specified goals, social innovations that aim to tackle complex societal challenges such as mental health demands a systemic approach, very different set of data and real time information to enable adaptive management throughout the life cycle of collaborative and multi-agent interventions (portfolio management).
name: Agirre Lehendakaria Center

The club believes that football can be used a force for good - not only in the local community but in wider society. The club promotes values of leadership, solidarity, integrity, respect and creavity, whilst working across 5 key areas: health & wellbeing, rehabilitation, educations & empowerment, anti-discrimination & inclusion and climate justice & sustainability.
name: Bohemian Football Club

The organisation operates through a network of locally branded sites and is run by volunteer Game Coordinators who are responsible for managing and delivering its activities. The organisation is supported by an advisory board including representatives from UEFA, the FAI, HSE, and Sport Ireland.

label: Irish Homeless Street Leagues
description: Founded in 2004, IHSL is a volunteer driven non profit organisation that uses the power of football to transform the lives of people who have found themselves affected by social exclusion. Through our nationwide leagues we create positive change by motivating, inspiring and developing people who have experienced homelessness, alcohol and substance abuse, or are ex-offenders, long term unemployed and refugees. The annual All Ireland Finals tournament brings together teams from our nationwide leagues to compete and teams are chosen to represent Ireland in the annual international Homeless World Cup.
name: Irish Homeless Street Leagues

label: Sanctuary Runners
description:
The Sanctuary Runners enable Irish residents to run alongside, and in solidarity with, migrants, asylum seekers and refugees thereby fostering friendship, positivity and respect while bringing greater awareness to the migration system. 

Established in 2018, SR has groups across Ireland and is  expanding internationally. The model allows communities to get to know each other, fosters community integration and improves physical and mental health.
name: Sanctuary Runners

label: Open Specturrm
description:
OPEN SPECTRUM is a parent-run activities club for children and young adults to the age of 30 years who are on the autistic spectrum in South Dublin. Our club was founded in 2013 by Helen Norris and Victoria White, two parents of teens on the spectrum who met at an autism-friendly event. They felt their children should have the same opportunities to take part in after-school activities that their “normal” children had but could not find a club to serve their needs so they decided to set one up.

https://openspectrum.ie/about-us/
name: Open Specturrm
